coredata.dc:title: Preparation of AgInS2 quantum dots and their application for Pb2+ detection based on fluorescence quenching effect
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Tong
Shi, Yiyi
Guo, Jie
Guo, Meixian
Yan, Ya
coredata.dc:description:
Water soluble AgInS2 quantum dots (AIS QDs) were successfully prepared by an environmentally friendly one-pot water phase method using glutathione (GSH) as the stabilizing agent. The crystal structure, morphology, size, chemical states, and optical properties of as-prepared AIS QDs were characterized through X-ray diffraction (XRD), Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR), Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis) and Photoluminescence spectrometer (PL). Factors affecting the fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs including reaction solution pH value, fluorescence test temperature and concentration of AIS QDs were investigated. The fluorescence stability of AIS QDs and the selectivity of AIS QDs toward Pb2+ detection was also evaluated. Under optimized test conditions, three excellent linear relationships
( (
F 0
− F ) /
F 0
= 27.938
C
P
b
2 +
− 0.002
( R = 0.998 )
,
(
F 0
− F )
/
F 0
= 10.362
C
P
b
2 +
− 0.005
( R = 0.997 )
,
(
F 0
− F )
/
F 0
= 6.554
C
P
b
2 +
− 0.240
( R = 0.9993
) were achieved between the concentration of Pb2+ and the relative fluorescence quenching amount of AIS QDs in the range of 9–96 nM, 48–434 nM and 193–530 nM, respectively. The detection limit was 4 nM and the recovery was in the range of 92.2%–102.6%. Our method had wide detection range, excellent selectivity, high sensitivity and easy operability, and could be applied for the content determination of Pb2+ in water. The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of AIS QDs by Pb2+ was discussed in detail and the dynamic quenching mechanism of light-induced electron transfer was put forward.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AgInS2 quantum dots
Fluorescence quenching
Pb2+
Glutathione

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21563003 ) and Research Team of Dali University for the Synthesis and Application of Micro/Nanomaterials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structure of glutathione.
XRD pattern of AIS QDs.
UPS spectrum of AIS QDs.
XPS spectra of AIS QDs. Survey spectrum (A) and high-resolution XPS spectra of Ag 3d (B), In 3d (C), S 2p (D), C 1s (E) and O 1s (F).
STEM images of AIS QDs.
UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of AIS QDs aqueous solution.
Stability curves of as-prepared AIS QDs.
Fluorescence spectra of AIS QDs prepared under different pH conditions. The inset showed the plot of fluorescence intensity versus pH.
Fluorescence spectra of AIS QDs measured at different temperatures.
Fluorescence spectra of the diluted solution prepared with different volumes of AIS QDs solution.
Fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs solution containing 0.06 μg/mL Pb2+ at different interaction time.
The linear curves between the relative fluorescence quenching amount of AIS QDs (0.20 mL (a), 0.40 mL (b), 0.60 mL (c)) and Pb2+ concentration.
The histogram of fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs containing different metal ions.

FTIR spectra of GSH, AIS QDs stabilized by GSH and AIS QDs containing Pb2+ (A), the pictures of fresh (left) and two months old (right) AIS QDs aqueous solution in nature light (B), UV–vis absorption spectra of AIS QDs and AIS QDs containing 0.08 μg/mL Pb2+ (C), and the Stern-Volmer plots of F
0
/F value versus concentration of Pb2+ under three test temperatures (D).
High resolution XPS spectra of Ag 3d (A), In 3d (B), S 2p (C) and Pb 4f (D) of AIS QDs containing Pb2+.
The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of AIS QDs by Pb2+.
Atomic percentages of the elements of sample.
Linear equations of different volume quantum dot solutions.
Comparison between the proposed method and other reported methods for Pb2+ detection.
Results of percent recovery of standard addition experiment (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Preparation of AgInS2 quantum dots and their application for Pb2+ detection based on fluorescence quenching effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Dali University, Dali, 671000, Yunnan, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, Dali University, Dali, 671000, Yunnan, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Water soluble AgInS2 quantum dots (AIS QDs) were successfully prepared by an environmentally friendly one-pot water phase method using glutathione (GSH) as the stabilizing agent. The crystal structure, morphology, size, chemical states, and optical properties of as-prepared AIS QDs were characterized through X-ray diffraction (XRD), Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR), Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis) and Photoluminescence spectrometer (PL). Factors affecting the fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs including reaction solution pH value, fluorescence test temperature and concentration of AIS QDs were investigated. The fluorescence stability of AIS QDs and the selectivity of AIS QDs toward Pb2+ detection was also evaluated. Under optimized test conditions, three excellent linear relationships
( (
F 0
− F ) /
F 0
= 27.938
C
P
b
2 +
− 0.002
( R = 0.998 )
,
(
F 0
− F )
/
F 0
= 10.362
C
P
b
2 +
− 0.005
( R = 0.997 )
,
(
F 0
− F )
/
F 0
= 6.554
C
P
b
2 +
− 0.240
( R = 0.9993
) were achieved between the concentration of Pb2+ and the relative fluorescence quenching amount of AIS QDs in the range of 9–96 nM, 48–434 nM and 193–530 nM, respectively. The detection limit was 4 nM and the recovery was in the range of 92.2%–102.6%. Our method had wide detection range, excellent selectivity, high sensitivity and easy operability, and could be applied for the content determination of Pb2+ in water. The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of AIS QDs by Pb2+ was discussed in detail and the dynamic quenching mechanism of light-induced electron transfer was put forward.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals (HMs) contamination has become one of the most widespread and serious problems in the aquatic environment around the world. It is widely believed that the impact of heavy metal pollution on healthy life is increasingly serious [1–3]. HMs are characterized by their wide distribution, various forms, difficult degradation and high toxicity [4]. They enter the environment, being absorbed by human body via direct contact or food chain [5]. HMs can accumulate and combine with proteins in the human body, giving rise to irreversible changes of protein structure, having a severe impact on basic functions of tissue cells, and ultimately causing a series of diseases. Lead pollutants have been recognized as one of the ten major types of pollutants threatening human health in the world. When blood lead concentration in the body exceeds the dose of 5 μg/L, it is liable to bring about mental disabilities, anemia, memory loss and cancer [5–7], which means that the harm of heavy metal lead to the human body cannot be ignored. In drinking water, 50 μg/L of maximum allowable limit of Pb is deemed safe by the Word Health Organization, whereas less than 15 μg/L is adopted by the United States Environmental Protection Agency [8]. At present, several mature methods for Pb2+ determination have been developed. For instance, atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [7–12], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [9,11,12], electrochemical technique [7,10–12] and colorimetric [7] etc. Nevertheless, these traditional approaches have some disadvantages such as complicated sample processing, time-consuming and expensive cost. Hence, it is of necessity for researchers to establish a green, fast, and handy technique for Pb2+ detection.

In recent years, Quantum Dots (QDs) have been widely used in the quantitative detection of heavy metals ions in water [13,14]. QDs, also known as semiconductor nanocrystals with less than 10 nm in size, are a sort of special material. In virtue of quantum size effect, quantum confined effect, surface effect and quantum tunneling effect, they have a variety of unique properties in optics, electricity as well as magnetism, and have been widely applied in several fields such as bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, medical diagnoses, drug delivery and photocatalysis [15–21]. Currently, there are many studies of binary QDs those are made up of Ⅱ-Ⅵ or Ⅳ-Ⅵ semiconductor elements including CdSe, CdTe and PbS. Although they have a great deal of exceptional optical properties, the practical application is limited greatly due to the inclusion of harmful elements [22]. In recent years, ternary quantum dots have attracted countless researchers' attention for low toxicity such as CuInS2, CuInSe2 and AgInS2 [15]. Among them, AgInS2 is a typical representative because of outstanding luminescent properties, broad excitation band, narrow emission band, big Stocks’ shift, long fluorescence lifetime, high fluorescence emission and high photostability [23–28]. Additionally, the photoelectric performance of AIS QDs can be changed by adjusting their sizes, regulating the composition and surface modifications [26,28]. Thus, it is promising to replace binary quantum dots containing chromium and lead with AIS QDs.

Up to now, the preparation methods of AIS QDs have been mainly divided into two methods: the organic phase method and water phase method. QDs obtained by organic phase method are lipophilic and need to be converted into hydrophilic QDs before they are utilized to detect metal ions in water. Therefore, we choose water phase method to prepare water soluble AIS QDs. GSH (a tripeptide compound formed by the condensation of cysteine, glutamic acid and glycine through peptide bonds, can participate in many activities of the body, and the chemical structure is shown in Fig. 1 .) was selected as the stabilizer because it contained many polarity functional groups such as carboxyl, amino, mercapto and amide groups, which could interact with nanocrystals.

In our previous experiments, we found Pb2+ could quench the fluorescence of AIS QDs. As a result, the fluorescence quenching effect is expected to be applied to the quantitative detection of Pb2+, which has not been reported in the relevant literature. In this work, water soluble AIS QDs were successfully prepared by an environmentally friendly one-pot water phase method using GSH as stabilizing agent. Good linear relationships were achieved between the concentration of Pb2+ and the relative fluorescence quenching amount of AIS QDs. The detection limit was low, and the recoveries were satisfactory. The developed method in our work could be applied to determine the Pb2+ content in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: All chemical reagents used in the experiment were of analytical grade without further purification. Silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.8%), lead (Ⅱ) nitrate (Pb(NO3)2, 99.0%), potassium chloride (KCl, 99.5%), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5%) and zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnSO4⋅7H2O, 99.5%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Isopropyl alcohol ((CH3)2CHOH, 99.7%), nitric acid (HNO3, 65.0–68.0%) and magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl8⋅6H2O, 98.0%) were purchased at Xilong Science. Indium nitrate hydrate (InN3O9⋅xH2O, 99.99%) was acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium sulfide (Na2S⋅9H2O, 98.0%) was obtained from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Glutathione (GSH, 98%) was purchased at Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Reagents Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96%) was purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagents Factory. Manganese sulfate (MnSO4⋅H2O, 99.0%) was purchased at Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Calcium chloride hexahydrate (CaCl2⋅6H2O, 95.0%) was obtained at Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Cobalt chloride (CoCl2⋅6H2O, 99.0%) was purchased from Third Branch of Tianjin Chemical Reagent Sixth Factory. Barium chloride dihydrate (BaCl2⋅2H2O, 99.5%) was obtained at Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd. Sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO3, 99.5%) was purchased at Jiangsu Qiangsheng Functional Chemical Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water with a specific resistivity greater than 18.25 MΩ cm was used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of AIS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: GSH was used as a stabilizer to prepare water soluble AIS QDs by a clean and facile one-pot water phase method. Firstly, a known amount of GSH powder and 150 mL ultrapure water were put into a 200 mL beaker (the molar ratio of GSH to Ag was 20). When GSH was completely dissolved, 1.00 mL AgNO3 (0.10 mol/L) and 4.00 mL InN3O9·xH2O (0.20 mol/L) were dropped into the beaker and stirred continuously for 15 min. Under magnetic vigorous stirring, the pH value of mixture solution was adjusted with NaOH (0.50 mol/L). Ten minutes later, 8.00 mL fresh Na2S⋅9H2O (0.10 mol/L) aqueous solution was dropped into the mixture solution under the condition of continuous stirring. When the mixed solution became bright yellow, it was transferred into a three-necked flask and heated to 120 °C in an oil bath for 120 min. After the reaction was accomplished, the mixed solution was cooled to room temperature naturally. Isopropyl alcohol was added into the reaction solution to wash and precipitate the AIS QDs (a volume ratio of 1.5:1), and the resulting mixture was exposed to centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 6 min. The obtained precipitate was stored at room temperature and away from light. To avoid introducing errors, the preparation and preservation of AIS QDs should avoid contacting with oxygen in the air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal structure of as-prepared AIS QDs was determined with a Bruker D8 X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, Bruker). The morphology, size and lattice spacing of as-synthesized AIS QDs were observed on a FEI Talos F200X Scanning transmission electron microscope (STEM, Thermo Scientific). The surface elements and the chemical states of the samples were analyzed by an Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The excitation source was Al Kα (hν = 1486.6 eV) and a monochromator was used, the electron emission angle was 32°, and the analyzed sample area was 500 μm. The work function value of AIS QDs was measured by ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The optical absorption capability of the samples was recorded with a TU-1901 Double-beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–Vis, China). Infrared spectra of the AIS QDs were obtained on a TJ270-30 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer with an ATR accessory (FTIR, China). The Photoluminescence spectroscopy was collected by a RF-5301PC spectrophotometer (PL, Shimadzu).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH value of the reaction solution on the fluorescence of AIS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the influence of pH value of the reaction solution on the fluorescence intensity of as-prepared AIS QDs, AIS QDs synthesized under different pH values were prepared into 1.58 × 10−4 g/mL aqueous solutions. 0.60 mL AIS QDs aqueous solution was taken and diluted to 5.00 mL with ultrapure water. The resulting diluent was placed in the dark for fluorescence measurements (λex = 450 nm, slit width = 15 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of test temperature on the fluorescence of AIS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: A thermostat temperature water bath was installed on the fluorescence spectrophotometer to control the fluorescence test temperature. 6.00 mL AIS QDs aqueous solution (1.58 × 10−4 g/mL) was diluted to 50.00 mL with ultrapure water. The fluorescence measurement was carried out according to the steps described in part 2.4, and the test temperature was set at 22 °C (room temperature), 30 °C, 40 °C, 50 °C, 60 °C, 70 °C and 80 °C, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the amount of AIS QDs on the fluorescence of AIS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: 0.10, 0.20, 0.30, 0.40, 0.50 and 0.60 mL AIS QDs aqueous solution (1.58 × 10−4 g/mL) were diluted to 5.00 mL with ultrapure water, respectively. The obtained dilute solutions were used for the determination of fluorescence spectra according to the steps illustrated in part 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction time between Pb2+ and AIS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: 12.00 mL AIS QDs aqueous solution (1.58 × 10−4 g/mL) and 6.00 mL Pb2+ standard solution (1.00 μg/mL) were added into 100.00 mL volumetric flask and diluted to 100.00 mL with ultrapure water. According to the steps described in part 2.4, the fluorescence spectra of the diluted solution were measured every 10 min until the spectra no longer changed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pb2+ using AIS QDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Standard Pb2+ solutions (1.00 μg/mL) of different volumes were added into 5.00 mL volumetric flask containing 0.20 mL, 0.40 mL and 0.60 mL AIS QDs aqueous solution (1.58 × 10−4 g/mL), respectively, which were diluted to 5.00 mL with ultrapure water to prepare 5.00 mL test solution. The fluorescence determination method was described in section 2.4. The fluorescence quenching efficiency was calculated by the following equation:
(1)
Q u e n c h i n g
e f f i c i e n c y =
(
F 0
− F )
/
F 0
= Δ F /
F 0

Herein, F
0 represented the initial fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs, F represented the fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs in presence of Pb2+, and ΔF was fluorescence quenching amount of AIS QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard addition recovery experiment
ce:section.ce:para:
0.00 mL, 0.36 mL, 0.45 mL and 0.54 mL standard Pb2+ solution (1 μg/mL) were added into volumetric flask containing 1.20 mL AIS QDs aqueous solution (1.58 × 10−4 g/mL) and 0.45 mL sample solution (1 μg/mL) to prepare 10.00 mL aqueous solution for fluorescence test. The fluorescence measurement was carried out according to the method described in part 2.4. The recovery of standard addition was calculated according to equation (2):
(2)
R e c o v e r y
( % )
=
(
C 2
−
C 1
)
/
C 3

Where, C
1 , C
2 and C
3 were the concentration of the sample, spiked sample and spiked amount, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of AIS QDs for Pb2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The metal ions solution (Pb2+, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+) and 1.20 mL AIS QDs aqueous solution (1.58 × 10−4 g/mL) were added into volumetric flask to prepare 10.00 mL aqueous solution for the fluorescence measurement. The concentration of Pb2+ was 0.30 μg/mL, K+ and Na+ concentrations were 520 times that of Pb2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, and Mg2+ concentrations were 5 times that of Pb2+, Zn2+ concentration was 2.5 times that of Pb2+, Mn2+ and Co2+ concentrations were the same as that of Pb2+. For comparison, the fluorescence spectra of AIS QDs in absence of metal ions were also determined under the same conditions. The fluorescence determination method was described in section 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Characterization of AIS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The information of crystal structure of as-prepared AIS QDs was analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2 , the diffraction peaks located at 28.4°, 37.1°, 44.5° and 52.6° were corresponding to (121), (202), (320) and (322) crystallographic plane of the orthorhombic phase AgInS2 (JCPDS NO. 25–1328), indicating that the prepared samples were orthorhombic phase AgInS2.

XPS is by far the most commonly used technique in areas of materials science, chemistry, and chemical engineering to assess surface chemistry, bonding structure, composition of surfaces and interfaces [29]. In this paper, XPS was also applied to analyze the surface elements and the chemical states of AIS QDs. Reliable XPS analyses require that the binding energy (BE) scale is calibrated, and C 1s peak of adventitious carbon (AdC) is widely used for BE referencing in XPS. Recent studies by G. Greczynski and L. Hultman show that the magnitude of C 1s shifts caused by the substrate is larger than typical chemical shifts, thus calibrating the BE scale using C 1s peak of AdC is not justified. The possible remedy is a complementary measurement of the sample work function (ϕ SA). The C–C/C–H component of the C 1s spectrum is set at 289.58 –ϕ SA eV and all other core-levels are shifted accordingly, which make chemical state determination have better accuracy as compared with the status quo [30–32]. Fig. 3 showed UPS spectrum of AIS QDs. From Fig. 3, the work function value (4.48 eV) of AIS QDs was calculated through thermo avantage software. Therefore, the C 1s peak for referencing was set at 285.1 eV based on the formula (289.58 –ϕ SA), and all other core-levels shifted accordingly. The corrected XPS spectra were shown in Fig. 4 . It could be obviously observed from Fig. 4A that AIS QDs were mainly composed of Ag, In, S, C, N and O elements. The atomic percentages of these elements were listed in Table 1 . The atomic% of oxygen element was 22.18%, which was more than that in glutathione (16.2%), suggesting that the oxygen element in AIS QDs came not only from GSH, but also from the air. Oxygen from carboxyl group and acyl group of GSH made AIS QDs have better water soluble, while the oxygen from air could capture photo-generated electrons and form superoxide radicals (⋅O2 −), which would lead to the fluorescence quenching of AIS QDs, thus introducing the experimental errors. Therefore, in subsequent studies, the preparation and preservation of AIS QDs should be in a vacuum environment or in an inert atmosphere to avoid contacting with the oxygen in the air. In the high resolution XPS spectrum of Ag 3d (Fig. 4B), there were two peaks centered at 368.1 eV and 374.1 eV, which were assigned to the Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively. In Fig. 4C, the two peaks located at 445.0 eV and 452.5 eV were attributed to In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2, respectively. In Fig. 4D, the binding energies of 161.6 eV and 162.7 eV were ascribed to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2. According to the NIST X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy Database and references [33,34], the valence state of Ag, In and S were +1, +3 and −2, respectively. Fig. 4E displayed that C 1s spectrum presented three peaks at 284.9 eV, 285.7 eV and 288.5 eV, which corresponded to sp3 C–C, C–O or C–N and O–CO groups, respectively [35]. The O 1s peaks were divided into three peaks at 531.8 eV, 533.8 eV and 535.8 eV (Fig. 4F), the first two peaks corresponded to CO and O–H [36], and the third one might arise from the adsorption of oxygen molecules.

STEM was employed to observe the microscopic morphology, size and lattice spacing of AIS QDs. In Fig. 5 A, it could be observed that the dispersity of AIS QDs was excellent. Fig. 5B showed that the AIS QDs particles were spherical shape with around 2.6 nm in size. Lattice stripes could be clearly observed from Fig. 5C (the magnifying part of the rectangular area in Fig. 5B), indicating the crystallinity nature of as-prepared AIS QDs. The lattice spacing was 0.242 nm and 0.173 nm in the inset of Fig. 5C, corresponding to the (202) and (322) crystal plane of orthorhombic structure AIS QDs. According to the crystal zone law, the crystal zone axis of the two crystal planes was [
2 ‾
1 2], which was parallel to the two crystal faces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of AIS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The light absorption capacity and emission intensity of the AIS QDs aqueous solution were evaluated by using an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fig. 6 showed the UV–vis absorption spectra (black) and fluorescence spectra (red) of the diluted solution with different volumes AIS QDs aqueous solution. The increase in absorbance of the AIS QDs aqueous solution was accompanied by the increase in volume. AIS QDs solution had good absorption in the ultraviolet region, the absorption onset was about 430 nm, and there was no obvious absorption peak. Similar result had been reported by other research groups [37,38]. The possible reasons could be attributed to either sole or combined factors of a broad size distribution and an unequal composition distribution as well as a unique electronic property of I-III-VI group ternary QDs [39]. Fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs ascended with increasing of volume and the position of the maximum emission peak did not shift.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of AIS QDs solution
ce:section.ce:para: Whether QDs could be widely utilized depended on their many characteristics. Among them, the stability played a significant role in the development of QDs for practical applications. We evaluated the stability of as-prepared AIS QDs through monitoring the fluorescence spectra of AIS QDs and the results were shown in Fig. 7 . The relative fluorescence intensity (red) and the position of maximum emission peak (blue) of AIS QDs were almost unchanged within 60 days, which suggested that the prepared AIS QDs aqueous solution was of good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH value of reaction solution on the fluorescence of AIS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs was largely affected by the pH value of reaction solution. The impact of pH value on the fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs was studied, and the results were shown in Fig. 8 . The fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs increased gradually with the increase of the pH value ranging from 5.03 to 7.12, which was due to the gradual deprotonation process of the functional groups of the stabilizer. These functional groups bonded with atoms on the surface of AIS QDs, effectively passivating the surface defects of AIS QDs, thereby enhancing fluorescence emission. The fluorescence intensity reached the highest when pH value was 7.12. When pH value exceeded 7.12, the fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs decreased with the increase of pH value, which was attributed to the presence of excessive OH− in the reaction solution, which interfered with bonding between the stabilizer and AIS QDs, resulting in forming a large number of surface defects, thus enhancing non-radiative recombination effects and reducing the fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs. Based on the above experimental results, 7.12 was selected as the optimal pH value for the preparation of AIS QDs in subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of test temperature on the fluorescence of AIS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectra of AIS QDs measured at different temperatures were displayed in Fig. 9 . It could be observed that the fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs decreased gradually and the maximum emission wavelength redshifted slightly with increasing the measure temperature. The redshift might be due to the change of the band structure of AIS QDs caused by the thermal expansion effect and the electron-phonon scattering effect [40]. The decrease of fluorescence intensity might be due to the decrease of carrier concentration in AIS QDs energy level with the increase of temperature [40]. Based on the above results, the determination of fluorescence spectrum was carried out at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the amount of AIS QDs on the fluorescence of AIS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of the diluted solution prepared with different volumes AIS QDs solution were shown in Fig. 10 . The emission intensity increased with increasing the volume of AIS QDs aqueous solution, and the maximum emission wavelengths did not shift, all centered at 596 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction time between Pb2+ and AIS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of interaction time between Pb2+ and AIS QDs on the fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs was investigated, and the results were shown in Fig. 11 . The fluorescence intensity of the AIS QDs decreased gradually with prolonging the interaction time until the interaction time reached 110 min, the fluorescence intensity no longer changed with time, indicating that the interaction between Pb2+ and AIS QDs reached equilibrium. Therefore, in the subsequent experiments, 110 min was selected as the interaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard curve and the linear equation
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 12 , the fluorescence quenching efficiency ΔF/F
0 exhibited an excellent linear response toward Pb2+ concentration using different volumes of AIS QDs solutions. The linear ranges, linear equations, and the correlation coefficients of standard curves were shown in Table 2 , which suggested that the detection range could be further expanded by using AIS QDs solution with different volumes. The detection limit (LOD) was 4 nM (calculated by the equation: LOD = 3σ/S, the σ (0.0019 μg/mL) and S were the standard deviation of samples containing 0.06 μg/mL Pb2+ solution (n = 10) and the slope of the linear equation, respectively), which was far less than the safety value considered by the World Health Organization (50 μg/L) and the United States Environmental Protection Agency (15 μg/L) [8]. Moreover, compared with other literature (Table 3 ), the limit of detection obtained in this work was low.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Percent recovery of standard addition
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the accuracy of our method, recovery percentage of standard samples experiment was evaluated. The results obtained were summarized in Table 4 . The recovery percentage of Pb2+ was in the range of 92.2%–102.6%, indicating that the method established in our work could be used for Pb2+ detection in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of AIS QDs for Pb2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of AIS QDs in presence of interfering metal ions were measured. For comparison, the fluorescence spectra of AIS QDs without metal ions were also measured under the same conditions. The results were shown in Fig. 13 . It was observed that Pb2+ drastically quenched the fluorescence of AIS QDs (Nearly 81%) compared with other metal ions. K+, Na+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and Zn2+ exhibited negligible effect on the fluorescence of AIS QDs, while the fluorescence of AIS QDs was quenched by Mn2+ (23%) and Co2+ (12%), respectively. It was worth noting that NaHCO3 solution (NaHCO3 was represented by X in Fig. 13) could eliminate the interference of Mn2+ and Co2+ on the fluorescence of AIS QDs by forming precipitates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of AIS QDs by Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to understand the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of AIS QDs by Pb2+, FTIR spectra of GSH, AIS QDs and AIS QDs in presence of Pb2+ were studied and the results were displayed in Fig. 14 A. The characteristic peaks of GSH at 3341 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of –OH, 3250 cm−1 by –NH, 3125 cm−1 and 3020 cm−1 by the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of –NH (from zwitterionic NH3 +), 2524 cm−1 by the stretching vibration of –SH, 1710 cm−1 by CO, 1657 cm−1 and 1536 cm−1 by amide I and amide II, 1596 cm−1 and 1392 cm−1 by the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of COO, respectively [26]. The peaks of 2361 cm−1 and 2330 cm−1 in GSH were derived from carbon dioxide in the atmosphere. The FTIR spectra of AIS QDs showed that O–H stretching vibration peak at 3431 cm−1, carbon dioxide peaks at 2324 cm−1 and 2355 cm−1, CO (amide I) at 1611 cm−1, N–H (amide II) deformation vibration peak at 1511 cm−1 and COO symmetric stretching vibration peak at 1385 cm−1, while the peaks of –SH at 2524 cm−1, –NH at 3250 cm−1, 3125 and 3020 cm−1, CO at 1710 cm−1 disappeared, which implied that GSH successfully bounded onto the surface of AIS QDs through these groups. In addition, there were hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups and amino groups on the surface of AIS QDs, which made AIS QDs have exceptional water solubility and could be preserved for several months without precipitation (Fig. 14B). The infrared spectrum of AIS QDs containing Pb2+ was almost the same as that of AIS QDs, indicating that there was not chemical bonding between AIS QDs and Pb2+. UV–vis absorption spectra of AIS QDs and AIS QDs containing Pb2+ were obtained (Fig. 14C). The absorption curves were almost unchanged, suggesting that the quenching effect did not occur in the ground state. Based on the analysis results of infrared spectroscopy and ultraviolet spectroscopy, we preliminarily speculated that the fluorescence quenching of AIS QDs by Pb2+ was caused by the dynamic quenching effect. To further confirm this, variable temperature experiments were implemented. Fig. 14D showed the Stern-Volmer plots of F
0
/F value versus concentration of Pb2+ under different test temperatures. Based on Stern-Volmer equation (equation (3)), the fluorescence quenching constant Ksv (the slope of the curve) could be obtained. Ksv were 8.9524, 9.9296 and 14.1332 for 303.15 K, 313.15 K and 323.15 K, respectively, which increased with increasing the test temperature, manifesting that the fluorescence quenching of AIS QDs by Pb2+ was dynamic quenching.
(3)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ]

Here, F
0 represented the fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs, F represented the fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs containing Pb2+, Ksv was the fluorescence quenching constant, and [Q] was the concentration of quencher.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: Orthorhombic AIS QDs stabilized by GSH with stable fluorescence intensity and high selectivity for Pb2+ detection was prepared via an eco-friendly and economical one-pot water phase method. The as-prepared AIS QDs were of spherical particles with about 2.6 nm in size and good dispersity in water. Based on the fluorescence quenching effect of AIS QDs by Pb2+, good linear relationships were achieved between the concentration of Pb2+ and the relative fluorescence quenching amount of AIS QDs in the range of 9–530 nM. Moreover, the linear concentration range of Pb2+ detection could be adjusted by varying the amount of AIS QDs aqueous solution. The detection limit was 4 nM and the recovery was in the range of 92.2%–102.6%. Our method established in this work was of wide detection range, excellent selectivity, high sensitivity and easy operability, and could be applied for the content determination of Pb2+ in water. The possible fluorescent quenching mechanism of AIS QDs by Pb2+ might be the dynamic quenching mechanism of light-induced electron transfer.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21563003) and Research Team of Dali University for the Synthesis and Application of Micro/Nanomaterials.

Adv. 6 (2016) 69977-69983. https://doi.org/10.1039/C6RA10836A.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective visual detection of Pb (II) ion via gold nanoparticles coated with a dithiocarbamate-modified 4'-aminobenzo-18-crown-6
Microchim. Acta
